metadata.source_title: The two faces of Agentic AI: weapon and shield

text:
The world of cybersecurity is at a tipping point. Agentic AI – artificial intelligence in which systems operate autonomously and make decisions without human intervention – is fundamentally changing how both defenders and attackers operate. Unlike generative AI, which relies on predefined instructions, these “agents” can independently initiate, execute and improve actions based on what they learn. Recent research from Palo Alto Networks, an American cybersecurity company, shows how dramatic this shift is. Researchers managed to simulate a full ransomware attack in just 25 minutes using Agentic AI – a hundred times faster than traditional methods. “The scenario they outline is disturbingly realistic,” says Matthijs Mons, cybersecurity specialist at ABN AMRO. “Criminals will develop agents specifically tailored for targeted attacks.”
From science fiction to reality.

metadata.source_title: The two faces of Agentic AI: weapon and shield

text: This leads to a striking paradox, revealed by a survey by SailPoint (an American technology company focused on identity and access management) of 353 cybersecurity professionals worldwide. While 96 percent of respondents view agents as an increasing security threat, a whopping 98 percent of organizations plan to expand their use of Agentic AI in the coming year. The research shows that 82 percent of companies already use agents, with more than half reporting that they access sensitive data on a daily basis. Despite this, only 44 percent have policies in place to protect these agents from external threats. Perhaps most alarming, nearly one in four companies reported that agents can be manipulated into giving away access credentials. “The use of this technology is a risk in itself,” warn Hans van Haarlem and Steven Ottenhoff of MMOX, a Dutch cybersecurity consultancy.
